channel.title: NPR Topics: Science
channel.description: The latest health and science news. Updates on medicine, healthy living, nutrition, drugs, diet, and advances in science and technology. Subscribe to the Health & Science podcast.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: Covering the Artemis II mission
item.description: Covering the Artemis II mission was a dream assignment for one NPR science correspondent.
item.content:encoded: Covering the Artemis II mission was a dream assignment for one NPR science correspondent.
item.dc:creator: Nell Greenfieldboyce

item.title: Former MIT president says the US is losing the innovation race to China
item.description: NPR's Rob Schmitz speaks with L. Rafael Reif, former president of MIT, about his recent essay in Foreign Affairs, "America Is Losing the Innovation Race: Why the Future of Science Might Be Chinese."
item.content:encoded: NPR's Rob Schmitz speaks with L. Rafael Reif, former president of MIT, about his recent essay in Foreign Affairs, "America Is Losing the Innovation Race: Why the Future of Science Might Be Chinese."
item.dc:creator: Rob Schmitz

item.title: Trump and Congress cut funding for Planned Parenthood. Can Botox keep it afloat?
item.description: After President Trump and Congress cut certain Medicaid funding to Planned Parenthood in last year's budget, some clinics have started offering aesthetic services, including Botox, to stay afloat.
item.content:encoded:
After President Trump and Congress cut certain Medicaid funding to Planned Parenthood in last year's budget, some clinics have started offering aesthetic services, including Botox, to stay afloat.

(Image credit: Tracy Barbutes for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Laura Fitzgerald

item.title: A real-life Kraken stalked the seas of the late Cretaceous
item.description: Researchers discovered evidence of enormous Kraken-like creatures who hunted in the seas some 100 million years ago, competing with large apex predators.
item.content:encoded:
Researchers discovered evidence of enormous Kraken-like creatures who hunted in the seas some 100 million years ago, competing with large apex predators.

(Image credit: Yohei Utsuki/Department of Earth and Planetary Sciences, Hokkaido University)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: Before sci-fi was everywhere, this pioneering magazine championed 'scientifiction'
item.description: The name didn't stick. The fan communities did.
item.content:encoded:
The name didn't stick. The fan communities did.

(Image credit: Amazing Stories)
item.dc:creator: Chris Klimek

item.title: 'Self-aware' robots can learn complex tasks by watching humans. Is that a good thing?
item.description: Scientists say they've made a key breakthrough that would allow robots to figure out complex tasks on their own, but experts say it raises questions about how much risk comes with letting robots be in charge of their own learning.
item.content:encoded:
Scientists say they've made a key breakthrough that would allow robots to figure out complex tasks on their own, but experts say it raises questions about how much risk comes with letting robots be in charge of their own learning. 

(Image credit: Malte Mueller)
item.dc:creator: Katia Riddle

item.title: In cities, wild things are hiding everywhere — if you put on your 'nature eyes'
item.description: NPR's Ailsa Chang talks with Amy Jaecker-Jones of the Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County about a worldwide community science project happening this weekend — the City Nature Challenge.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Ailsa Chang talks with Amy Jaecker-Jones of the Natural History Museum of Los Angeles County about a worldwide community science project happening this weekend — the City Nature Challenge.
item.dc:creator: Gurjit Kaur

item.title: AI's a suck up. Research shows how it flatters and suggests we're not to blame
item.description: The AI models and chatbots that we interact with tend to affirm our feelings and viewpoints — more so than people do, with potentially worrisome consequences.
item.content:encoded: The AI models and chatbots that we interact with tend to affirm our feelings and viewpoints — more so than people do, with potentially worrisome consequences.
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: Instead of civil war, a naked mole rat colony changed queens peacefully
item.description: These matriarchal rodents often have bloody succession wars to replace their queen. But in a colony in California, Queen Tere ceded the throne to her daughter, Arwen, without violence.
item.content:encoded:
These matriarchal rodents often have bloody succession wars to replace their queen. But in a colony in California, Queen Tere ceded the throne to her daughter, Arwen, without violence.

(Image credit: Evgeniya Moskova/iStockphoto)
item.dc:creator: Pien Huang

item.title: A mine despoiled the beauty of the rainforest. This Goldman Prize winner took action
item.description: "We women are the land guardians and keepers," says Theonila Roka Matbob of Papua New Guinea, recognized for her efforts to repair the environmental and social harms caused by a copper and gold mine.
item.content:encoded:
"We women are the land guardians and keepers," says Theonila Roka Matbob of Papua New Guinea, recognized for her efforts to repair the environmental and social harms caused by a copper and gold mine.

(Image credit: Goldman Environmental Prize)
item.dc:creator: Gabrielle Emanuel
